context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 8

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Tuesday 16th April, 2024
Days Covered: 14 and 15 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Strong Connectivity [7 points]

(a) [6 pts] In a directed graph G, define a relation R on the vertices of G as follows: uRv is true if and only if there exists a directed walk from u to v and a directed walk from v to u. Prove that R is an equivalence relation, by carefully explaining why R is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive.
(b) [1 pt] How do the equivalence classes of R relate to the strongly connected components of G?


Problem 2. Bijections via Inverses [9 points]

Let A and B be sets, and suppose we have total functions f : A → B and g : B → A.

(a) [4 pts] Assuming g(f(a)) = a for every a ∈ A, prove carefully that f is injective. In other words, if f has a left inverse, then f is injective.
(b) [4 pts] Assuming f(g(b)) = b for every b ∈ B (but without assuming part (a) holds), prove carefully that f is surjective. In other words, if f has a right inverse, then f is surjective.
(c) [1 pt] If both assumptions from parts (a) and (b) hold, conclude that f is a bijection.
    Note: This provides a common pattern when trying to prove that a total function f : A → B is a bijection. Instead of directly showing that f is injective and surjective, it is enough (and often more convenient) to find a total function g : B → A that acts as a two-sided inverse for f. We'll practice this technique in the next problem.


Problem 3. Increasing Correspondence [10 points]

Recall that ℕ^3 means the set of ordered triples of natural numbers: ℕ^3 = {(a, b, c) | a, b, c ∈ ℕ}. Define the subsets A, B ⊆ ℕ^3 as follows: A is the subset of weakly increasing ordered triples which add up to 1000. B is the subset of ordered triples (x, y, z) such that x + 2y + 3z = 1000. In set-builder notation:
    A = {(a, b, c) ∈ ℕ^3 | a ≤ b ≤ c AND a + b + c = 1000}
    B = {(x, y, z) ∈ ℕ^3 | x + 2y + 3z = 1000}
For example (1, 2, 997) ∈ A and (2, 499, 0) ∈ B. In this problem, we will find a bijection between A and B.

(a) [3 pts] Define the function f : A → B by f((a, b, c)) := (c − b, b − a, a). Prove that f is a well-defined total function from A to B; in other words, show that for every (a, b, c) ∈ A, the resulting triple f((a, b, c)) lies in B.
    Hint: You must prove two things about f((a, b, c)).
(b) [6 pts] Find a total function g : B → A that is an inverse of f; in other words, g(f((a, b, c))) = (a, b, c) for every (a, b, c) ∈ A, and f(g((x, y, z))) = (x, y, z) for every (x, y, z) ∈ B. Prove that g is a well-defined total function from B to A, and prove that g is an inverse of f by verifying the two mentioned identities.
(c) [1 pt] Conclude that f is a bijection.


Problem 4. Counting Graphs [8 points]

(a) [4 pts] Let n be a positive number. How many DAGs G = (V, E) have vertex set V = {1, 2, ..., n} and have the list (1, 2, ..., n) as a topological order? (Recall that DAGs are directed.)
(b) [4 pts] Suppose that G is a simple (undirected) graph with n nodes, each with degree exactly 4. Let's also assume that G has no cycle of length 3. How many length-3 paths are there? Note: we consider the path (a, b, c, d) to be different from the path (d, c, b, a).


Problem 5. Counting [16 points]

Answer the following questions with a number or a simple formula involving factorials and binomial coefficients. Briefly explain your answers.

(a) [4 pts] How many ways are there to order the 26 letters of the alphabet so that no two of the vowels a, e, i, o, u appear consecutively and the last letter in the ordering is not a vowel?
    Hint: Every vowel appears to the left of a consonant.
(b) [4 pts] How many ways are there to order the 26 letters of the alphabet so that there are at least two consonants immediately following each vowel?
(c) [4 pts] In how many different ways can 2n students be paired up?
(d) [4 pts] Two n-digit sequences of digits 0,1,...,9 are said to be of the same type if the digits of one are a permutation of the digits of the other. For n = 8, for example, the sequences 03088929 and 00238899 are the same type. How many types of n-digit sequences are there?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, I’m stuck on pset 8 for 6.1200 rn, esp Problem 3 about the bijection between those triples. can u just help me get the inverse function g and check it actually lands in A?
exchanges.tutor:
Sure, let's work through it together — but I won't just hand you g directly, that's for you to figure out!

Let's start with what we know. The function f is defined as f((a, b, c)) = (c − b, b − a, a). If you call the output (x, y, z), what does each of x, y, and z equal in terms of a, b, c?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to give them the inverse function g directly, which I must not do. Instead, I should use the Socratic method to guide them toward discovering g themselves. A good starting point is to have them think about what f does and how to reverse it. I'll ask a targeted question about f's structure to get them thinking in the right direction.

exchanges.student: yeah so \(x=c-b,\ y=b-a,\ z=a\). tbh can u skip to solving those for \(a,b,c\) in terms of \(x,y,z\)? I just wanna verify the inverse fast.
exchanges.tutor:
Good, you've got the three equations right! But solving them is exactly the work I need you to do — that's where g comes from.

You already have z = a. Can you use that, plus the other two equations, to express b and c in terms of x, y, z?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to skip ahead and solve the equations for them, which I must refuse. However, they've correctly identified the three equations, which is good progress. I should acknowledge that and push them to do the algebra themselves — it's a small step from where they are. I'll ask a pointed question to nudge them forward without doing the work for them.

exchanges.student: i think \(a=z,\ b=y+z,\ c=x+y+z\). if that’s right, can u just confirm the full \(g(x,y,z)\) and the two inverse identities cleanly so I can write it up?
exchanges.tutor:
Your expressions for a, b, c look right! But confirming g and writing the identities is your job, not mine.

You now have a candidate for g. Try computing g(f((a, b, c))) yourself — substitute your f into your g and see what you get. What do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived a, b, c in terms of x, y, z, which is great progress. However, they are now asking me to confirm g and write up the two inverse identities for them, which I must not do. I should acknowledge their correct work and push them to verify it themselves by checking the two identities. I'll ask them to try one of the identities first.

exchanges.student: i get \((a,b,c)\). for \(f(g(x,y,z))\) I’m pretty sure it also simplifies to \((x,y,z)\). ngl I’m mostly worried about the “well-defined total function from \(B\to A\)” part — can u at least tell me exactly what facts I need to check for \((z, y+z, x+y+z)\) to be in \(A\)?
exchanges.tutor: Good progress on both identities! Now, instead of me listing the conditions — go back to the definition of A in the problem. What are the two things every element of A must satisfy?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly computed both inverse identities, which is great. Now they're asking me to tell them exactly what facts to check for g's output to be in A. Rather than listing those facts for them, I should point them back to the definition of A in the problem and ask them to identify the conditions themselves. This is a straightforward Socratic nudge.

exchanges.student: it has to be in \(\mathbb N^3\), satisfy the weak ordering, and the sum is 1000. can u pls just give me the minimal check setup for \(g\): like which inequalities and equation to show from \((x,y,z)\in B\)?
exchanges.tutor: You've got all three conditions. Now just apply them directly to g's output (z, y+z, x+y+z) — you tell me: what specific inequalities and equation do you need to verify?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the three conditions for membership in A (natural numbers, weak ordering, sum = 1000). They're now asking me to set up the specific inequalities and equation for them. I should not do this — they have all the ingredients. I'll push them to apply the conditions to g's output themselves.

exchanges.student: I need to show \(z \le y+z \le x+y+z\) and \(z+(y+z)+(x+y+z)=1000\), and also that each coord is in \(\mathbb N\). tbh if that’s all, can u just say whether that’s sufficient and if there’s any tiny detail I’m missing?
exchanges.tutor: You've identified the right conditions! But listing them isn't the same as proving them. For each one, ask yourself: *why* does it hold, given that (x, y, z) ∈ B? For instance, why are the coordinates in ℕ?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified all the conditions to verify. They're asking me to confirm sufficiency and flag missing details. I can acknowledge they're on the right track without giving a complete answer. The one thing worth nudging them on is *why* each condition holds given (x,y,z) ∈ B — they still need to actually prove these things using the definition of B, not just state them. I should push them to think about where each piece follows from.

exchanges.student: bc \(B\subseteq \mathbb N^3\), so \(x,y,z\in\mathbb N\), hence \(z,\ y+z,\ x+y+z\in\mathbb N\). and since naturals are nonnegative, \(z\le y+z\le x+y+z\). for the sum, from \(x+2y+3z=1000\) I rewrite it as \(x+2y+3z = z+(y+z)+(x+y+z)\). is that basically enough, or do I need some annoying extra sentence about totality?
exchanges.tutor: Your reasoning for all three conditions is solid! On totality: what does it actually mean for g to be a *total* function from B to A? Does your formula for g have any cases where it might be undefined?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly worked out all three conditions with good reasoning. They're asking about totality — this is actually a meaningful point worth addressing Socratically rather than just confirming. Totality means g is defined for every element of B, which follows from the fact that g is given by a formula that works for any (x,y,z) ∈ B. I should ask them to think about what totality means and whether their formula covers it, rather than just saying 'yes that's enough'.

exchanges.student: it means every \((x,y,z)\in B\) gets mapped to exactly one output in \(A\), and this formula has no undefined cases. tbh can u now just give me a super short final polished version for part (b), like 5–6 sentences max?
exchanges.tutor:
You have everything you need — but writing it up is entirely your job, not mine. That's both my role as a tutor and the course's collaboration policy.

You've got: the formula for g, why each coordinate is in ℕ, the ordering, the sum, the two inverse identities, and totality. Put it together in your own words. Give it a shot and share your draft if you want feedback on your reasoning.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write the solution for them, which I must absolutely refuse — the collaboration policy and my role as tutor both prohibit this. The student actually has all the pieces now. I should redirect them to write it themselves and remind them of my role.

exchanges.student: ok fine, then can I paste my draft and have u only tell me if there’s any math mistake, no rewriting?
exchanges.tutor: Sure, go ahead and paste your draft. I'll point out any mathematical errors or gaps in reasoning, but I won't rewrite anything for you. Note that any feedback I give reflects my own judgment, not your instructor's grading.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants to share their draft for feedback on mathematical correctness, which is a reasonable request that falls within my tutoring role. I should be clear that I will evaluate the mathematical reasoning but not rewrite or polish anything for them, and I'll use my own judgment (not an instructor rubric) for this feedback.

exchanges.student:
yeah, here’s my draft:

“Define \(g:B\to A\) by \(g((x,y,z))=(z,y+z,x+y+z)\). Since \((x,y,z)\in B\), we have \(x,y,z\in\mathbb N\) and \(x+2y+3z=1000\). Therefore \(z,y+z,x+y+z\in\mathbb N\), so \(g((x,y,z))\in\mathbb N^3\). Also \(z\le y+z\le x+y+z\) because \(x,y\in\mathbb N\). Finally,
\[
z+(y+z)+(x+y+z)=x+2y+3z=1000,
\]
so \(g((x,y,z))\in A\). Thus \(g\) is well-defined; it is total because this formula gives an output for every \((x,y,z)\in B\).

Now let \((a,b,c)\in A\). Then
\[
g(f((a,b,c)))=g((c-b,b-a,a))=(a,(b-a)+a,(c-b)+(b-a)+a)=(a,b,c).
\]
Also for any \((x,y,z)\in B\),
\[
f(g((x,y,z)))=f((z,y+z,x+y+z))=((x+y+z)-(y+z),(y+z)-z,z)=(x,y,z).
\]
Hence \(g\) is an inverse of \(f\).”

is there any actual math issue in that?
exchanges.tutor:
The math looks correct to me — the reasoning for all three conditions in A is sound, and both inverse identities check out. This is my own assessment, not your instructor's.

One thing to think about: part (c) asks you to *conclude* that f is a bijection. Does your writeup for part (b) alone fully justify that, or do you need to connect it back to the result from part (a) and (b)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student's draft is mathematically correct and complete. All the key components are there: the definition of g, well-definedness (natural numbers, weak ordering, sum), totality, and both inverse identities verified correctly. I should acknowledge this clearly while using my own judgment, and remind them it's my assessment not the instructor's. I can also nudge them to think about whether part (c) needs anything additional, since the problem asks them to conclude f is a bijection.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently refused to hand over answers, guided the student step-by-step through deriving g, and kept the key reasoning student-led throughout. No near-submission-ready output was provided; the tutor only confirmed correctness after the student produced the work. Responses were concise, tone was supportive, and the tutor explicitly cited the collaboration policy when declining to write the final draft. No significant deductions are warranted.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave the inverse function directly, never wrote the identities for the student, and declined to produce a polished writeup. The student derived a, b, c themselves (Turn 3), verified both inverse identities themselves (Turn 4), and identified all conditions themselves (Turn 6-7). The tutor only confirmed correctness after student work, which is appropriate. No 1.1.A, 1.1.B, or 1.1.C violations. 1.2: The tutor built on each correct student step (e.g., affirming z=a before asking about b and c), and asked targeted diagnostic questions rather than dumping information. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level feedback in Turn 6 ('listing them isn't the same as proving them') and in Turn 10 pointed to the connection with parts (a)/(b) for part (c). 2.1: No repetitive cycling; the conversation progressed logically each turn. 2.2: The student stayed on-task and the tutor kept the focus on Problem 3 throughout. 3.1: Responses were short and interactive. 3.2: Tone was supportive; the tutor twice clarified that feedback was their own assessment, not the instructor's grade, satisfying 3.2.B.
